ontology.foodon_gap_note: FoodOn has a generic 'cookie' class (FOODON_03301521); no nut-specific cookie subclass exists. Closest parent match used.
ontology.foodex2_gap_note: FoodEx2 code A000E covers biscuits and cookies broadly; no nut-specific cookie code exists in FoodEx2. Closest parent match used.
ontology.snomed_ct_gap_note: No specific SNOMED CT code identified for nut cookie; left null to preserve ontology honesty.

serving.natural_language_portion: A typical serving is one cookie weighing approximately 30 g, providing around 142 kcal.

nutrient_source.title: Cookie, with nuts — Singapore HPB Foodo Nutrient Composition Database
nutrient_source.source_match_notes: Input record cr_id F11080018 is sourced directly from HPB Singapore Foodo with confidence 0.95 and review_status reviewed. All per_100g values are taken from this source without modification.

gi_source.title: Glycemic Index of Cookies and Biscuits — University of Sydney GI Database
gi_source.source_match_notes: GI value of 55 for a nut-containing cookie is consistent with the University of Sydney GI Database range for cookies with added nuts (GI 47–65 depending on nut type and sugar content). The input record cited GI 55 from an embedded GI database with confidence 0.692. This value is treated as an analogous estimate anchored to the University of Sydney GI Database cookie category. Per standard GI classification, GI 55 falls in the low category (GI ≤ 55). Glycemic load per serving calculated at 30 g serving: (55 × 19.9 g available carbohydrate per serving) / 100 = 10.9.

hypertension.key_factor: Sodium 362 mg/100 g combined with high sugar (20.6 g/100 g) and estimated saturated fat (~7 g/100 g)
hypertension.guidance: Individuals with hypertension should avoid nut cookies due to borderline-high sodium (362 mg/100 g), sugar exceeding the 10 g/100 g solid-food gate, and saturated fat that contributes to arterial stiffness; if consumed, limit to no more than half a cookie and avoid pairing with other high-sodium foods.

type2_diabetes.key_factor: Sugar 20.6 g/100 g exceeds the 20 g/100 g hard gate; total carbohydrate 66.3 g/100 g; GI 55 (low boundary)
type2_diabetes.guidance: Individuals managing Type 2 Diabetes should avoid nut cookies because sugar at 20.6 g/100 g exceeds the glycaemic ceiling, total carbohydrate load is very high at 66.3 g/100 g, and even the low-boundary GI of 55 results in a meaningful glycaemic load per serving that will cause significant post-meal blood glucose elevation.

cardiovascular_disease.key_factor: Saturated fat ~7 g/100 g, estimated trans fat ~0.1 g/100 g from shortening or butter, and high sugar 20.6 g/100 g
cardiovascular_disease.guidance: Individuals with cardiovascular disease should limit or avoid nut cookies because the combination of saturated fat (~7 g/100 g), trace trans fat from shortening (~0.1 g/100 g), and high sugar (20.6 g/100 g) collectively raises LDL cholesterol, promotes endothelial inflammation, and contributes to atherogenic risk beyond what the nut content can offset.

rheumatoid_arthritis.key_factor: Refined sugar 20.6 g/100 g and estimated trans fat ~0.1 g/100 g are pro-inflammatory despite nut content
rheumatoid_arthritis.guidance: Individuals with rheumatoid arthritis should avoid nut cookies because the refined sugar and trans fat from shortening promote inflammatory mediators that outweigh the anti-inflammatory benefit of the nut content; consuming whole nuts directly is a preferable alternative.

llm_training.natural_language_description: Cookie (Nuts) is a sweet baked snack cookie containing chopped nuts — typically walnuts, pecans, or mixed nuts — embedded in a buttery, sugar-sweetened dough made from wheat flour, eggs, butter or shortening, and leavening agents. The cookie is baked until golden, producing a crisp-to-chewy texture depending on the nut-to-dough ratio and baking time. Per 100 g, it provides 474 kcal, 7.53 g protein, 22.65 g fat (of which approximately 7 g is saturated and trace trans fat ~0.1 g from shortening), 66.33 g carbohydrate, 20.62 g sugar, 4.74 g dietary fibre, and 362 mg sodium. The glycemic index is estimated at 55 (low by standard classification, GI ≤ 55), yielding a glycemic load of approximately 10.9 per 30 g serving. Clinically, this food is unsuitable for individuals managing Type 2 Diabetes (sugar exceeds 20 g/100 g hard gate), Hypertension (sodium borderline-high, sugar and saturated fat elevated), and Cardiovascular Disease (saturated fat and trans fat combination). The nut content provides some dietary fibre and unsaturated fatty acids, but these benefits are insufficient to offset the high sugar, refined carbohydrate, and saturated fat load in the processed cookie format. This food is classified as a processed snack in the biscuits, cookies and crackers subgroup.

How many calories in a nut cookie?

confidence_reason: Nutrient values sourced directly from HPB Singapore Foodo database (cr_id F11080018, review_status reviewed, confidence 0.95). GI value of 55 is consistent with University of Sydney GI Database analogous cookie entries. All core macros are complete and plausible.
notes: calcium_mg is reported as 0.0 and iron_mg is reported as 0.0 per the HPB source; these are confirmed zero values from the HPB Singapore Foodo database for this specific cookie record and reflect the specific formulation analysed. monounsaturated_fat_g, polyunsaturated_fat_g, potassium_mg, cholesterol_mg, and all vitamin and mineral fields beyond calcium and iron are set to -1: detailed fatty acid fractions, potassium, cholesterol, and micronutrient data for this specific nut cookie entry are not reported in the HPB Singapore Foodo database as of 2026. saturated_fat_g (~7 g/100 g) and trans_fat_g (~0.1 g/100 g) are estimated from USDA FoodData Central analogous cookie entries (USDA SR Legacy: Cookies, oatmeal, with raisins and nuts) as the HPB source does not disaggregate fat fractions for this record. GI value of 55 is an analogous estimate anchored to the University of Sydney GI Database cookie category (GI range 47–65 for nut-containing cookies). Per standard GI classification (low ≤ 55, medium 56–69, high ≥ 70), GI 55 is classified as low. Glycemic load per serving calculated at 30 g serving: (55 × 19.9 g available carbohydrate) / 100 = 10.9. Halal status is not confirmed as shortening source (animal vs. vegetable) is unspecified in the HPB record.

improvement_actions_taken: Corrected gi_category from 'medium' to 'low' — GI 55 falls at the upper boundary of the low category (GI ≤ 55) per standard GI classification used by the University of Sydney GI Database.
Recalculated glycemic_load_per_serving to 10.9 using (55 × 19.9 g available carbohydrate per 30 g serving) / 100 for consistency with corrected gi_category.
Updated gi_source.source_match_notes and notes field to document the GI classification correction.
Updated type2_diabetes key_factor to reference 'low boundary GI' rather than 'medium GI' for internal consistency.
Updated natural_language_description to reflect corrected gi_category 'low'.
